---
title: "Math Interlude: Legendre Polynomials"
subtitle: "The natural basis functions for problems with azimuthal symmetry"
reading:
  boas: "Ch. 12 (§1–6)"
classes: math-interlude
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Math Interlude

This is a mathematical foundations section. The Legendre polynomials appear naturally when solving Laplace's equation in spherical coordinates with azimuthal symmetry. We develop their key properties here so they can be used fluidly in the physics sections.
:::

## Legendre's Equation

When separating Laplace's equation in spherical coordinates with azimuthal symmetry ($m = 0$), the $\theta$-dependent part satisfies:

$$
\frac{d}{d\theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\frac{d\Theta}{d\theta}\right) + \ell(\ell+1)\sin\theta\,\Theta = 0
$$

With the substitution $x = \cos\theta$ (so $x \in [-1,1]$), this becomes **Legendre's equation**:

$$
\frac{d}{dx}\!\left[(1-x^2)\frac{dP}{dx}\right] + \ell(\ell+1)P = 0
$$ {#eq-legendre-equation}

For the solution to be finite at $x = \pm 1$ (i.e., at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \pi$), $\ell$ must be a non-negative integer, and the solutions are the **Legendre polynomials** $P_\ell(x)$.

## The First Few Legendre Polynomials

$$
P_0(x) = 1
$$ {#eq-P0}

$$
P_1(x) = x
$$ {#eq-P1}

$$
P_2(x) = \frac{1}{2}(3x^2 - 1)
$$ {#eq-P2}

$$
P_3(x) = \frac{1}{2}(5x^3 - 3x)
$$ {#eq-P3}

$$
P_4(x) = \frac{1}{8}(35x^4 - 30x^2 + 3)
$$ {#eq-P4}

Note: $P_\ell(1) = 1$ and $P_\ell(-1) = (-1)^\ell$ for all $\ell$.

## Rodrigues' Formula

All Legendre polynomials can be generated from:

$$
P_\ell(x) = \frac{1}{2^\ell \ell!}\frac{d^\ell}{dx^\ell}(x^2-1)^\ell
$$ {#eq-rodrigues}

This is Rodrigues' formula. It's useful for proofs but not for computation.

## Generating Function

The same expansion that appears in the multipole setup (@eq-1-over-r-expansion) is actually the **generating function** for Legendre polynomials:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 2xt + t^2}} = \sum_{\ell=0}^{\infty} P_\ell(x)\, t^\ell, \quad |t| < 1
$$ {#eq-legendre-generating}

Setting $x = \cos\alpha$ and $t = r'/r$ (with $r' < r$) recovers the expansion of $1/|\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}'|$ in the multipole section.

## Orthogonality and Completeness

The Legendre polynomials are **orthogonal** on $[-1,1]$:

$$
\int_{-1}^{1} P_\ell(x)\, P_{\ell'}(x)\, dx = \frac{2}{2\ell+1}\,\delta_{\ell\ell'}
$$ {#eq-legendre-orthogonality}

They are also **complete**: any "reasonable" function $f(x)$ on $[-1,1]$ can be expanded as:

$$
f(x) = \sum_{\ell=0}^{\infty} c_\ell\, P_\ell(x)
$$ {#eq-legendre-expansion}

with coefficients:

$$
c_\ell = \frac{2\ell+1}{2}\int_{-1}^{1} f(x)\, P_\ell(x)\, dx
$$ {#eq-legendre-coefficients}

The factor $(2\ell+1)/2$ is the inverse of the normalization in @eq-legendre-orthogonality.

**This is how you extract multipole moments from a known potential on a sphere**: if $V(R,\theta) = f(\theta) = f(\cos\theta)$, then comparing with @eq-spherical-general-solution at $r = R$ gives you the coefficients $A_\ell$ and $B_\ell$ via @eq-legendre-coefficients.

## Recurrence Relations

Useful for computation:

$$
(\ell+1)P_{\ell+1}(x) = (2\ell+1)x\, P_\ell(x) - \ell\, P_{\ell-1}(x)
$$ {#eq-legendre-recurrence}

$$
(1-x^2)P_\ell'(x) = \ell[P_{\ell-1}(x) - x P_\ell(x)]
$$ {#eq-legendre-derivative-recurrence}

## Associated Legendre Functions

When azimuthal symmetry is broken ($m \neq 0$), the angular equation becomes the **associated Legendre equation**:

$$
\frac{d}{dx}\!\left[(1-x^2)\frac{dP}{dx}\right] + \left[\ell(\ell+1) - \frac{m^2}{1-x^2}\right]P = 0
$$ {#eq-associated-legendre-eq}

The solutions are the **associated Legendre functions**:

$$
P_\ell^m(x) = (-1)^m (1-x^2)^{m/2} \frac{d^m}{dx^m} P_\ell(x), \quad 0 \leq m \leq \ell
$$ {#eq-associated-legendre}

(For negative $m$: $P_\ell^{-m}(x) = (-1)^m \frac{(\ell-m)!}{(\ell+m)!} P_\ell^m(x)$.)

Their orthogonality relation is:

$$
\int_{-1}^{1} P_\ell^m(x)\, P_{\ell'}^m(x)\, dx = \frac{2}{2\ell+1}\frac{(\ell+m)!}{(\ell-m)!}\,\delta_{\ell\ell'}
$$ {#eq-associated-legendre-orthogonality}

These functions form the building blocks of the spherical harmonics (see the [spherical harmonics interlude](math-spherical-harmonics.qmd)).

## Reading Guide

| Source | Coverage |
|--------|----------|
| Boas Ch. 12 §1–3 | Legendre's equation; Legendre polynomials; Rodrigues' formula; generating function |
| Boas Ch. 12 §4–6 | Orthogonality; completeness; associated Legendre functions |
| Griffiths §3.3 | Legendre polynomials in the context of separation of variables |
